coredata.dc:title: Metal doped graphene oxide derived from Quercus ilex fruits for selective and visual detection of iron(III) in water: Experiment and theory
coredata.dc:creator: Tewari, Chetna
SanthiBhushan, Boddepalli
Srivastava, Anurag
Sahoo, Nanda Gopal
coredata.dc:description: Iron is one of the most abundant metals and an important micronutrient that needs to be consumed in balanced amounts. Excess consumption or deficiency of this micronutrient can have adverse effects on biological functioning of human organs. Hence, the selective detection of this micronutrient is very important. The present work reports a simple and selective sensing of iron (III) in aqueous solutions using potassium doped graphene oxide (K-doped GO), which is synthesized from Quercus ilex fruits via a greener route of solvo-hydrothermal method. The synthesis of K-doped GO is confirmed with the help of various characterisation techniques viz. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Raman Spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX), and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR).The Density Functional Theory (DFT) based first principle simulations are carried out to understand the interactions between K-doped GO and iron ions. The simulations reveal desorption of K-ions from the GO sheet due to the extremely high binding energy of iron ions on GO surface, which destabilizes the oxide groups as well as the potassium dopant. The desorbed K-ions from GO surface may react with water to form potassium hydroxide and subsequent quenching of fluorescence. Thus, the synthesized K-doped GO demonstrates its utility as a heavy metal ion sensor with detection limit of 0.345*10−7 M iron ions via spectrofluorometer. Moreover, this material is capable of detecting the presence of iron ions in water up to a limit of 7 ppm with naked eye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quercus ilex
Fluorescent material
Irons sensing
K-doped graphene oxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Fe(III)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Science and Technology, India
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors acknowledge the SUTRAM Project (Ref. No. DST/TM/WTI/WIC/2K17/82(G) ), funded by Department of Science and Technology, India .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Raman, (b) FT-IR, (c) XRD, and (d) TGA spectra of K-doped GO.
(a) TEM image, and (b) EDX data of K-doped GO.

a). Visualization of colour change of a 1 mM iron (III) solution on addition of K-doped GO. b) Colour of the iron (III) solution having different iron ion concentrations before (top samples) and after (bottom samples) adding 10 μl K-doped GO. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
TEM and Atomic composition of the black residue obtained during iron sensing.
XRD data of the black residue obtained during iron sensing.
Top view and side view of the structures before and after structural relaxation for the 3 considered cases. Binding energy (EB) for each case is given in the respective image.
(a) UV-FL, (b) normalized graph, (c) UV, and (d)fluorescence at 365 nm of K-doped GO.
(a) The quenching of fluorescence of K-doped GO,(b)the interference study of K-doped GO in the presence of different metal ions, (c)Fluorescence intensity with respect to iron ion concentration.
Summary of various precurssor materials with their limit of detection for iron (III) ions.

row.entry: Seedless Grown ZnONanorods in 3 Dimensions Using Nickel Foam Substrate

head.ce:title: Metal doped graphene oxide derived from Quercus ilex fruits for selective and visual detection of iron(III) in water: Experiment and theory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PRS-Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Centre, Department of Chemistry, D.S.B. Campus, Kumaun University, Nainital, 263001, Uttarakhand, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . PRS-Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Centre, Department of Chemistry, D.S.B. Campus, Kumaun University, Nainital-263001, Uttarakhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, Indian Institute of Information Technology, Kottayam, Kerala, 686635, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, Indian Institute of Information Technology, Kottayam, Kerala - 686635, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Materials Research Group, CNT Lab, ABV - Indian Institute of Information Technology and Management, Gwalior, Madhya Pradesh, 474015, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ABV - Indian Institute of Information Technology and Management

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Advanced Materials Research Group, CNT Lab, ABV - Indian Institute of Information Technology and Management, Gwalior-Madhya Pradesh, 474015, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron is one of the most abundant metals and an important micronutrient that needs to be consumed in balanced amounts. Excess consumption or deficiency of this micronutrient can have adverse effects on biological functioning of human organs. Hence, the selective detection of this micronutrient is very important. The present work reports a simple and selective sensing of iron (III) in aqueous solutions using potassium doped graphene oxide (K-doped GO), which is synthesized from Quercus ilex fruits via a greener route of solvo-hydrothermal method. The synthesis of K-doped GO is confirmed with the help of various characterisation techniques viz. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Raman Spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX), and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR).The Density Functional Theory (DFT) based first principle simulations are carried out to understand the interactions between K-doped GO and iron ions. The simulations reveal desorption of K-ions from the GO sheet due to the extremely high binding energy of iron ions on GO surface, which destabilizes the oxide groups as well as the potassium dopant. The desorbed K-ions from GO surface may react with water to form potassium hydroxide and subsequent quenching of fluorescence. Thus, the synthesized K-doped GO demonstrates its utility as a heavy metal ion sensor with detection limit of 0.345*10−7 M iron ions via spectrofluorometer. Moreover, this material is capable of detecting the presence of iron ions in water up to a limit of 7 ppm with naked eye.

ce:section.ce:para: The ionic form of metals coordinate with the proteins, nucleic acids, hormones and vitamins that perform a key role in regulating the biochemical and physiological processes of living organisms (Lewis and Laemmli, 1982; Senthilnathan et al., 2014).Unbalanced intake of any metal content can raze the metabolism balance, hence the quantitative detection of metal ions is very important. Iron is an essential trace element which is mainly responsible for the transport of oxygen (O2) to the cells in humans and animals (Xu et al., 2014; Ying, 2014).The deficiency of iron ions may result in health issues such as fatigue, reduced immunity, anemia, kidney damage, liver issues, methemoglobinemia, heart diseases, diabetes, etc. The presence of iron above the permissible limit of 0.3 mg/L may also cause hemochromatosis leading to damage of liver and heart (Kumar et al., 2012).Many water bodies in the north Indian region contain high concentration of iron. Kumar et al. reported high iron concentrations in the water samples collected from the district of Moradabad (UP) in India. They collected a total of 64 samples, of which more than 50% have iron concentrations higher than the permissible limit. They reported the maximum observed iron level in surface water samples as 6294 ppb (according to bureau of Indian standards, the permissible upper limit of iron in drinking water is 300 ppb (Kumar et al., 2017)).Unfortunately, these water resources are being used by the local communities for drinking purposes, which signifies the need of developing simple and low cost techniques for the detection of Fe(III)ions in water bodies (Lohani and Lee, 2010).Several analytical techniques like atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry are available for ion detection. Though, these methods are sophisticated, they require tedious sample preparation steps. Fluorescent nanoparticles are fascinating class of nanomaterials with high sensitivity and good selectivity for metal ions (Zhang and Chen, 2014; Hu et al., 2010; Ju et al., 2014).The cost-efficiency and quick response of colorimetric assay makes this technique makes it an extensively preferable choice for heavy metal sensing in aqueous solutions (Li et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2014; Alizadeh et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2014).For instance, Yin et al. demonstrated a colorimetric based sensor for detection of Cu(II) ions through catalytic oxidation of L-cysteine (Yin et al., 2015).The detection of copper (II), lead (II), nickel (II) and Fe(III) through nanoparticles of gold and silver was demonstrated by scientists (Gao et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2013; Annadhasan et al., 2014; Sang et al., 2012; Lewa et al., 2020). Several researchers also reported the sensing of iron ions using composite nanomaterials (Zang et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2017; Pang et al., 2020), however, the precursor sutilized by the mare quite expensive. The synthesis of nanomaterials and nanoparticles from various kinds of solid wastes such as plastic waste and agro-waste have been reported elsewhere (Pandey et al., 2019; Tewari et al., 2019; Somanathan et al., 2015; Liou et al., 2020; Talebi et al., 2021; Baiocco et al., 2016; Skiba et al., 2019; Sinsinwar et al., 2018; Palanisamy et al., 2021). Although, various sophisticated iron detection techniques already exist in the market, most of them are quite expensive. Thus, here we aim to develop a unique and cost effective technique for selective and visual detection of iron ions in water, utilizing the K-doped GO synthesized from Quercus ilex fruits.

Quercus ilex was chosen as the raw material owing to its bulk availability in the forests of Himalayan region. A green and cost effective hydro-solvothermal method is used to synthesize the K-doped GO. Quercus ilex fruits contain high concentration of potassium, thus naturally K-doped GO sheets can be obtained from them. The concentration of other metals present in the Quercus ilex fruit is negligible in comparison to the concentration of potassium.It was previously reported that the electronic and chemical properties of carbon based nanomaterials can be tuned by doping (Tewari et al., 2019), and the doped carbon structures can be a great choice for detection of water pollutants. Hence, the attempts are made in this work to synthesize naturally K-doped GO and utilize the same for metal ion detection in aqueous solutions.

Waste fruits of Quercus Ilex (oak) were collected from forests of the Nainital region (Uttarakhand, India). Nylon filter paper (0.2 μm), teflon lined hydrothermal reactor, ethanol, and various salts (procured from Sigma Aldrich) such as sodium perchlorate, cobalt (II) perchlorate hexahydrate, potassium perchlorate, iron (III) perchlorate hydrate, zinc perchlorate hexahydrate, aluminium perchlorate nanohydrate, cesium perchlorate, manganese (II) perchlorate hydrate, barium perchlorate, lithium perchlorate, calcium perchlorate tetrahydrate, magnesium perchlorate, nickel (II) perchlorate hexahydrate, and silver perchlorate are utilized in the synthesis and sensing evaluation process.

K-doped GO is synthesized through hydro-solvothermal method using the double distilled water and ethanol as solvents. The white pulp obtained from the oak fruit has been mixed with the solvents, and heated at 200 °C for 2 days in hydrothermal reactor to form the graphitic sheets. Briefly, the collected waste oak fruits were subjected to rigorous washing using distilled water to eliminate the impurities such as dust particles, followed by the removal of cupule and pericap (outer layer).Thereafter, 10 g of the white part of oak fruit was crushed and then added double distilled water and ethanol in 1:1 ratio (20 ml each). Subsequently, the mixture is heated at 200 °C for 48 h inside an oven, to obtain supernatant-liquid which is brown in color along with black colored solid residue. In order to remove the larger particles, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min at room temperature. The obtained supernatant solution is filtered with a nylon based filter paper of 0.2 μm, and the resulting solution is dried at 100 °C to get K-doped GO. The K-doped GO exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV light and selective quenching of fluorescence in presence of iron (III) even at very low concentrations of 0.345*10−7 M. This material also helps in visual detection of iron (III) in water with naked eyeupto7ppm concentrations.

The quantum yield of the compound estimated in double distilled water is found to be 3.03%.The quantum yield is calculated using the Fluorescein as a standard, utilizing the expression given below:
QYC = QYR. (IC/AC).(AR/IR).(ηC/ ηR)2
Where, QYC and QYR represent quantum yield of sample and reference, respectively. A is optical density at excitation wavelength, and I is intensity of the luminescent spectra. Q is estimated using Fluorescein in 0.1 N NaOH solution (quantum yield from literature is 0.92 at 366 nm) as a standard for K-doped GO. The subscript letter ‘R’ representsa standard with well-known quantum yield, whereas the ‘C'represents sample in the given expression. The absorbance in 10 mm fluorescence cuvette has been considered below 0.02–0.05 at the excitation wavelength, so as to reduce the reabsorption effects.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of K-doped GO are obtained using Agilent Cary 100UV Spectrometry. The excitation and emission of the samples are obtained with the help of a spectrofluorometer from Varian Cary Eclipse. The emission and excitation band pass is set to 5 nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and FT-IR (PerkinElmer) measurements are carried out using the JEM-2100Finstrument having a field emission gun operated at 200 kV. Raman instrument (R.I. India, RIRM-LP1519 having 532 nm excitation wavelengths) was used, TEM microscopy of JEOL JEM 2100, and Thermogravimetry Analysis instrument of PerkinElmer having TGA 4000thermal analyzer was used to investigate morphology and chemical composition of the prepared material. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis has also been performed using Rigaku Miniflex-II instrument having Cu-Kα radiationof1.5418 Å wavelength (λ).

During the synthesis of K-doped GO from oak fruits using solvothermal process, the sp3 hybridized carbon atoms of the cellulosic part of oak fruits are converted into sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. This conversion process is referred to as aromatization process, through which the carbon atom stand to attain maximum stability as reported elsewhere (Pandey et al., 2019).Raman spectroscopy is utilized to confirm the synthesized GO and to evaluate its quality (Gupta et al., 2007; Ferrari, 2007; Ferrari et al., 2006).The Raman spectrum of K-doped GO (Fig. 1 a) shows two signature peaks namely D and G bands at 1345 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1, respectively (Graf et al., 2007; Casiraghi et al., 2007).The D and G bands correspond to the presence of disordered structure in the crystals of sp2 cluster, and E2gmode of graphitic structure for in-plane stretching of vibrational mode, respectively.

FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b) is used to determine the various functional groups present on the surface of K doped GOs. The spectra shows characteristic peaks at 3451 cm−1 (υstr of O–H), 2918 cm−1 (υstr of C–H), 1638 cm−1 (υstr of CO), 1440 cm−1 (υstr of CC), and 1120 cm−1which corresponds to epoxy and symmetrical ring distortion vibration. This confirms the presence of various oxygen based functional groups namely epoxy, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the K doped GOs (Loryuenyong et al., 2013; Sharma et al., 2017).

XRD technique is used to examine the characteristic diffraction peaks of GO at 2θ = 24.6, where the obtained broad peak is a result of potassium doping. This spectra revealed the amorphous nature of material (Johra et al., 2014).Further, the number of layers and the thickness of GO sheets is evaluated with the help of XRD data as described elsewhere (Pavoski et al., 2017).In this method, the number of layers is calculated by dividing the crystal size (C) with the thickness of a single graphene sheet (0.1 nm) added to the interlayer distance (d). Using Bragg's LawandScherrer equation, the interlayer distances (d) and crystal size (C) were calculated (Sahoo et al., 2014; Aladekomo et al., 1990).The crystal size of K-doped GO was found to be0.92 nm with inter-planar distance of 0.62 nm.The XRD data shows3-4 layers of K-doped GO. Further, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) is carried out to evaluate the nature of GO sheets, which revealed the weight loss of GO sheets in two phases viz. 180 °C to 325 °C and 500 °C–650 °C. The first phase of weight loss can be attributed to the removal of oxygen based functional groups from GO, whereas the second phase of weight loss can be attributed to the oxidation of carbonic skeleton of graphene nanosheets (Tewari et al., 2019). A 35% residual weight at 650°Cindicate the existence of various functional groups in K doped GO. The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) of K-doped GO displays the sheet like structure. In order to ratify the existence of potassium and other metal atoms in the synthesized GO, STEM-EDXS spectrum analysis is performed (Fig. 2b), where a uniform dispersion of the potassium dopant with 3.22 wt% is observed in the GO sheets. The measurements are repeated thrice to maintain accuracy in the results through self-consistent evaluation.

The process of utilizing the K-doped GOs as Fe(III) sensor is illustrated as follows. The fluorescence spectrum of 2 ml K-doped GO suspension is noted at the emission maxima with and without the metal ions. Various metal ions are added separately to the samples by diluting different salts viz. sodium perchlorate, cobalt (II) perchlorate hexahydrate, potassium perchlorate, iron (III) perchlorate hydrate, zinc perchlorate hexahydrate, aluminium perchlorate nanohydrate, cesium perchlorate, manganese (II) perchlorate hydrate, barium perchlorate, lithium perchlorate, calcium perchlorate tetrahydrate, magnesium perchlorate, nickel (II) perchlorate hexahydrate, and silver perchlorate. The K-doped GO exhibits emission maxima at 460 nm on excitation at 370 nm.The plot between 1-F/F0 v/s metals at 450 nm emission maxima is depicted in Fig. 8a, where F and F0correspond to emission intensity with and without the metal ions, respectively, at the emission maxima. For K-doped GO the value of 1-F/F0 is zero, whereas the same has changed on addition of Li(I) ions to 0.07, Na(I) ions to 0.07, Mg(II)ions to 0.1, Al(III) ions to 0.1, K(I) ions to 0.06, Ca(II)ions to 0.07, Mn(II)ions to 0.18,Fe(III) ions to 0.88, Co(II)ions to 0.14, Ni(II)ions to 0.28, Zn(II)ions to 0.1, Ag(I) ions to 0.1, Cd(II)ions to 0.1, Cs(I) ions to 0.14, and Ba(II)ions to 0.07. These values indicate the amount of quenching occurred in the fluorescence on addition of metal ions. Here, the irons ions stand out with the extremely high quenching of 0.88, which portray the K-doped GOs as iron detection probe.

The transparent water sample containing the Fe(III)ions quickly turned black (within a second) on addition of the synthesized K-doped GO (Fig. 3 a), which is not the case when K-doped GO is added to the pure (distilled) water. The detection process is repeated for wide range of concentrations (1–1000 ppm) of Fe(III)ions. The results show that this process is capable of visually detecting the iron ions over a concentration range of 7–1000 ppm (Fig. 3b). Thus, the quick response of the synthesized material towards the detection of iron can be treated as an iron ion detection probe. Moreover, this reaction leaves black sediment at the bottom after few minutes. To gain further insights of the sensing mechanism this sediment has been washed with distilled water and characterized using TEM-EDX, and XRD techniques.

Brief insights of the sensing mechanism can be obtained by comparing the atomic compositions of K-doped GO before (Fig. 2b) and after iron sensing (Fig. 4). From Fig. 2b, the atomic weight percentage of potassium in K-doped GO before iron sensing is 3.22%, which is reduced to a negligible value of 0.04% in the black residue that formed after addition of Fe(III) ions. The K-doped GO has no iron content, whereas the black residue has an iron concentration of 0.54 wt %. Moreover, the oxygen concentration of the K-doped GO reduced to 4.62 wt % from 51.28 wt %. To further evaluate and understand these reductions in Potassium and oxygen concentrations and the incorporation of iron ions into GO sheets after sensing process, we have executed the first-principle simulations based on Density Functional Theory (DFT).To gain better insights, the interactions between GO sheet and iron ion has been analyzed in the presence and absence of K-dopant (shown in Fig. 6 ). Case-1 of Fig. 6 shows the interaction between GO and Iron in the absence of K-dopant. Case-2 shows the substitutional doping of K-atom into GO. Case-3 shows the interaction between GO and Iron in presence of K-dopant.

The DFT simulations are performed using Synopsys-QuantumATK (Smidstrup et al., 2020), with the exchange correlation functional of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) within Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA) method. The van Der Waals interactions between GO sheet and Oxide groups/K-dopant/Fe-ions have been defined by the semi-empirical Grimme DFT-D2 correction (Stefan et al., 2006). The limited pseudo atomic orbitals with double zeta polarized basis sets are used to describe the valance electrons of the atoms. A large density mesh cutoff of 90 Hartree has been utilized for simulation accuracy. The geometry relaxations of 5*5 hexagonal super cells of GO are done using limited-memory Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (L-BFGS) quasi-Newton method (Liu et al., 1989),so that the forces on the atoms and stress on the super cell converge below the tolerance value of 0.05 eV/Å and 0.0006 eV/Å3, respectively. A Monkhorst-Pack grid of 10*10*1 k-points have been used for the geometry relaxations. To mimic the ionic behavior of Iron atom, a positive charge of +3 have been added to the super cell.

The strength of interaction for each case shown in Fig. 6 has been estimated with the help of binding energies (EB), calculated using the expression (1) given below,
(1)
E B
=
E
G O
o r
K − G O
S h e e t
+
E
F e
I o n
o r
K
a t o m
−
E
A d s o r b e d
G O
s h e e t
Where, E represents energy.

In general, a large binding energy denotes strong adsorption. From Fig. 6, the calculated binding energies for all the three cases are positive, signifying stable adsorption phenomenon. It is worth noting that the binding energies are obtained by subtracting the energy of product from the energy of reactants. In case-1 (case-3), the adsorption studies of iron with the GO sheet in the absence (presence) of K-dopant have been performed. The GO sheet computationally designed for the present simulations contain the oxide groups viz. Epoxy (R-O-R), Hydroxyl (R–OH), and Carboxyl (R–COOH). In case-1, the calculated binding energy for iron ion adsorption on GO sheet is 37.51eV, which indicates very strong chemisorption behavior. The geometry after structural relaxation shows that the iron ion binds chemically to both the carbon atoms and oxide groups of GO sheet. The interaction is so strong that the iron ion is able to pluck out the hydroxyl (R–OH) group out of the GO sheet. Simulation of various possible scenarios of case-1 indicated desorption of few oxide groups from the GO sheet irrespective of the location of iron ion on the sheet. This indicates the possible destabilization of oxide groups caused by the iron ion interaction. Thus, offers a confirmation of the reduction in oxygen concentration of K-doped GO after iron sensing, as observed experimentally earlier.

In case-2, a potassium dopant has been introduced into the pristine GO sheet. The binding energy of potassium adsorption on GO is noted as 2.61eV. Here, no such destabilization of oxide groups is witnessed, may be due to the normal value of binding energy of potassium atom rather than the extremely high value as observed for iron ion. Things turn fascinating when the iron ion is made to interact with K-doped GO as presented in case-3. The binding energy of iron ion in this case is noted as 41.30eV, which is even higher than case-1. The presence of iron ion not just destabilized the oxide groups but also the potassium dopant. Before interaction of iron ion with the K-doped GO, the potassium dopant is at an optimal distance of 2.76 Å above the sheet surface forming a trigonal pyramidal shape owing to its sp
3 hybridization. After interaction of iron ion with K-doped GO, the distance of potassium from the sheet surface is increased to a massive 9.85 Å. This confirms the release of potassium atoms from the K-GO sheet into water on sensing the iron ions, which was also observed experimentally. Moreover, desorption of potassium dopant from GO further enhanced the interaction strength of the latter with iron ions as seen from the very high binding energy of 41.30 eV. This may be attributed to the fact that as a positive potassium ion leaves K-doped GO in presence of iron ions and turns into potassium hydroxide in water (2K(s) +2H2O(l) → 2KOH(aq) + H2(g)), the redundant negatively charged GO strongly attracts the positive iron irons.

An excitation dependent emission behavior has been observed for the synthesized K-doped GO with the emission maxima at 430 nm when excited at 350 nm (Fig. 7 ).The emission behavior and spectroscopic aspects of the K-doped GO suspended in double distilled water are depicted in Fig. 7a, b & c. It displays strong absorption at the wavelengths of 230–380 nm with the λ-max at 270 nm attributed to π-π* transition (Fig. 7c, black line). This wide absorption of K-doped GO dispersed in water is due to the different particle size. When these particles are excited at 370 nm they show emission maxima at 465 nm (Fig. 7c blue line). To explore their excitation dependent emission behavior, several scans of the samples were taken and collected its emission response as shown in Fig. 7a, which clearly indicate their excitation dependent emission behavior. The normalized fluorescence spectra (Fig. 7b) display strong fluorescence red shift of dispersed K-doped GOs in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Fe(III)
ce:section.ce:para: The significant quenching of fluorescence intensity in case of iron indicates the selectivity of K-doped GO towards iron, even in the presence of other metal ions such as Li(I), Mg(II), Na(I), Al(III),K(I), Ca(II), Co(II), Mn(II), Ni(II), Ag(I), Zn(II), Cd(II), Cs(I) and Ba(II). The selectivity studies were carried out using fluorescence spectrophotometer which reveal that our K-doped GO can selectively detect Fe(III)ions (Fig. 8 ).The fluorescence properties of K-doped GO was quenched in presence of Fe(III)ion, while the quenching is negligiblein presence of other metal ions, which may be attributed to the remarkable coordination between hydroxyl group (R–OH)and Fe(III) ion of K-doped GO. To understand the quenching effect in a practical environment where several other metal ions can exist alongside the iron ions in a water body,the interference study has been performed (Fig. 8b), which reveal that the influence of other metal ions on the quenching associated with iron ions is almost negligible.

Fig. 8c shows the variation of fluorescence intensity with the metal ion concentration. Where, the fluorescence intensity is observed to decrease with the increasing Fe(III) concentration. The quenching in fluorescence efficiency can be described further with the help of Stern–Volmer plot having linear pattern (the linear correlation coefficient is 0.987)for the Fe(III) concentration range of 1*10−7 M to 10*10−7 M as shown in Fig. 8c. The detection limit was observed to be as low as 0.345*10−7 M (34.5 nM) using the formula 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation of blank sample signal and m is the slope of the linear fit. Table 1 presents the comparison of our work with various previous reports, where some materials have been synthesized for detection of iron (III).

To summarize, the present work reports a simple, direct as well as green approach for synthesis of K-doped GO using Quercus ilex as carbon source. The high potassium contents of the precursor helps in synthesizing naturally metal doped GO sheets. The presence potassium dopants and oxygen containing functional groups enable selective interaction of K-doped GO with the iron ions of water bodies. The process offers quick response with formation of black colored solution and subsequent formation of black residue at the bottom. Furthermore, the iron ion detection is also supported by the fluorescence quenching effect of the K-doped GO in the presence of Fe (III) ion. The method is observed to detect iron concentrations as low as 0.345*10−7 M.

The present work reports successful synthesis of highly fluorescent K-doped GO by hydrothermal method using Quercus Ilex fruits as a precursor, and the subsequent utilization of the same as a Fe (III) ion sensor. This work offers new insights into the synthesis of naturally K-doped GO via a greener and cost-effective route using Quercus ilex fruits as precursor, and ethanol as well as water as reagents. The synthesized material allows Fe (III) ion detection with naked eye, as the addition of these ions instantaneously turns the transparent K-doped GO water sample into a black colored solution. The Density Functional Theory (DFT) based first principle simulations are also carried out to understand the interactions between the K-doped GO and iron ions. The simulations reveal desorption of K-ions from the GO sheet due to the extremely high binding energy of iron ions on GO surface, which destabilizes the oxide groups and potassium dopant. The K-ions thus desorbed from the GO may react with water to form potassium hydroxide and subsequent quenching of fluorescence. Analysis of fluorescence quenching effect reveals the selective detection capability of this method towards iron ions, even in the presence of other metal ions. A linear reduction in the fluorescence intensity of K-doped GO is witnessed with increased Fe(III) concentration. This method is able to detect iron ion concentrations as low as 0.345*10−7 M, suggesting promising detection capability. Thus, the synthesized K-doped GO demonstrates its utility as a heavy metal ion sensor. We believe that the proposed sensing mechanism can be a game changer in the field of heavy metal ion detection upon further development.

Chetna Tewari: Manuscript preparation, experiment work. Boddepalli SanthiBhushan: DFT study/ theoretical explanation. Anurag Srivastava: DFT study/ theoretical explanation. Nanda Gopal Sahoo: Checking of manuscript, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge the SUTRAM Project (Ref. No. DST/TM/WTI/WIC/2K17/82(G)), funded by Department of Science and Technology, India.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abbasi et al., 2013

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iron (III) ion sensor based on the seedless grown ZnO nanorods in 3 dimensions using nickel foam substrate
J. Sens.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abbasi, M. A., Zafar H. I., Yaqoob K., Azam K., Omer N., Magnus W., 2013. Iron (III) ion sensor based on the seedless grown ZnO nanorods in 3 dimensions using nickel foam substrate. J. Sens. (2013), 1-7.
